front: Figure: this Flemish Habsburg monarch ruled simultaneously as King of Spain (Charles I) and Holy Roman Emperor in the early-to-mid 16th century
Figure: his empire included Naples, Burgundy, the Low Countries, Austria, Spain, and much of the New World
Figure: was the son of Joanna "the Mad" of Castile
Figure: was a patron of the painter Titian
Figure: inherited the Spanish throne in 1516 and was elected Holy Roman Emperor three years later, in 1519
work: Rise to Power & the Revolt of the Comuneros

front: Figure: borrowed millions of ducats from the Fugger family and this other Augsburg banking family to finance his imperial election
work: Rise to Power & the Revolt of the Comuneros

front: Figure: this chief early advisor, drawing on Dante's De Monarchia, shaped his imperial ambitions
back: Mercurino di Gattinara (advisor to Charles V)
work: Rise to Power & the Revolt of the Comuneros

front: Figure: governed the Low Countries from Coudenberg Palace with this aunt as regent
back: Margaret, Duchess of Savoy (regent for Charles V)
work: Rise to Power & the Revolt of the Comuneros

front: Event: this 1520-21 Castilian uprising against Charles V's absentee rule ended with the execution of leaders Bravo, Padilla, and Maldonado
work: Rise to Power & the Revolt of the Comuneros

front: Figure: faced a revolt from a seven-foot-tall Frisian pirate named Pier Gerlofs Donia
work: Rise to Power & the Revolt of the Comuneros

front: Battle: Charles V's army defeated and captured Francis I of France here in 1525, imprisoning him in the tower of Pizzighettone
work: Wars with Francis I of France

front: Event: after Pavia, Pope Clement VII organized this alliance (with Francis I, Venice, and Florence) against Charles V, whose forces then sacked Rome
work: Wars with Francis I of France

front: Event: after the Ottomans forced the Knights Hospitaller to retreat from Rhodes in 1522, Charles V granted the order this island in exchange for an annual tribute of one falcon
back: Malta (granted by Charles V to the Knights Hospitaller)
work: Ottoman Rivalry with Suleiman the Magnificent

front: Event: Charles V's disastrous Algiers expedition (with admiral Andrea Doria) and Suleiman's admiral Hayreddin Barbarossa's Siege of Nice were part of this rivalry, which included the Battle of Preveza
back: Charles V's naval rivalry with Suleiman the Magnificent
work: Ottoman Rivalry with Suleiman the Magnificent

front: Event: Charles V's rising power spurred Francis I to form this alliance with the Ottomans, which jointly captured Nice from Charles in 1543
work: Ottoman Rivalry with Suleiman the Magnificent

front: Event: Charles V convened this 1521 assembly (allied with Pope Leo X) and declared Martin Luther an outlaw
Event: Charles V battled this coalition of Lutheran princes (grown from the earlier League of Torgau) over the Tetrapolitan Confession, defeating them at Muhlberg under the 3rd Duke of Alba
Event: this league of German cities helped Charles V suppress the Peasants' Revolt and later overthrow Ulrich of Wurttemberg
Event: this 1555 peace, signed at a German city, led Charles V to divide his possessions between the "Austrian" and "Spanish" branches of his family
Figure: Hernan Cortes and Francisco Pizarro led their conquests of the Aztec and Inca empires under this ruler's reign
Figure: founded this administrative body governing the Spanish colonies after the conquests of Pizarro and Cortes
back: Council of the Indies (founded under Charles V)

front: Event: Charles V commissioned this debate where Bartolome de las Casas argued for indigenous rights against Juan Gines de Sepulveda, partly under pressure from the bull Sublimis Deus
Law: Charles V issued this 1542 legislation outlawing Native American slavery and severely restricting the encomienda system
back: New Laws (1542, issued by Charles V)

front: Figure: during the Mixton War, his court applauded a captured leader's speech on human dignity before he had the man thrown back into a dungeon to die
Figure: this anatomist, who overturned Galenic anatomy, served as personal physician to Charles V and his son Philip II
Figure: supplied five ships to this explorer's attempt to circumnavigate the world
Figure: abdicated all his titles in 1556 and retired to a monastery at this Spanish location
Figure: his 1556 abdication split his empire between his son Philip II (Spain) and this brother (Austria and the imperial title)
back: Ferdinand I, Holy Roman Emperor (Charles V's brother)

front: Figure: dealt harshly with this order of migratory shepherds, founded in 13th-century Castile, while managing Spain's economy
Painting: Charles V "receives the world" in this allegorical portrait
Painting: this equestrian portrait of Charles V by Titian depicts him carrying a spear in the aftermath of the Battle of Muhlberg
Painting: this Hans Makart painting, depicting Charles V entering a city surrounded by nude virgins, was denounced in the U.S. by Anthony Comstock
back: Entry of Charles V into Antwerp (Hans Makart)

front: Painting: Charles V kneels across from Diego Deza at the bottom right of this Francisco de Zurbaran painting
back: The Apotheosis of Saint Thomas Aquinas (Francisco de Zurbaran)
